mnemonics: Think of Granularity as GRAIN SIZE: Granularity (GRAIN) is the SIZE of data item locked (e.g., entire database, table, page, row).
keyPoints: Granularity refers to the size of the data item chosen as the unit of protection in a locking protocol.
Multiple Granularity Locking (MGL) allows a transaction to lock data items of different sizes (e.g., a table and then a specific row within it), increasing system concurrency.
MGL uses intention locks (IS, IX) to signal that a transaction intends to request a more granular lock on a subset of the data item, preventing conflicting coarse-grained locks.
commonMistakes: Confusing lock modes: Assuming an intention lock (like IX) is enough to perform an operation; it only signals intent and a finer-grained lock is still needed.
Thinking finer granularity (e.g., row-level) is always better; it has higher overhead due to managing more locks, so the optimal size depends on the transaction's behavior.
